_meta.purpose: Authoritative module-by-module build roadmap for Deep in the Heart: A Lone Star Journey. Content synthesized from each lab's overview.body and overview.objectives in pages/labs/json/lab-*.json (as of 2026-05-18).
_meta.manualSyncNote: When a lab's objectives or game-state arc change materially, the corresponding card in this JSON needs to be updated by hand.

overview.label: Project Overview
overview.intro_html:
This page is your project plan for Deep in the Heart: A Lone Star Journey. It walks the full semester — what you build in every lab, what each lab adds to the game, and what the game does at the end. Use it to orient yourself before a module, to see where the lab you’re working on fits in the arc, or to remind yourself why a particular concept matters for the bigger picture.

Every lab BUILDs a feature, ENHANCEs it, then MASTERs it. By Module 5 you’re refactoring your own code from week 1 into a class-based architecture. You don’t start over. Every line you write in Module 1 still runs (in some form) in Module 5.
overview.endStateHeading: By the end of the semester, the game will:
endState_html: Load city distances, city info, hazards, and Texas trivia from text files at startup — add a row to a file, add content to the game
Let players save and reload partway through a road trip and resume where they left off
Use a D20 for mileage per turn, a TRUE/FALSE trivia gate to validate destination choices, and random hazards on the road
Handle bad input gracefully — no crashes, no ValueErrors, friendly retry messages
Track session statistics across multiple games (total miles, cities visited, hazards faced, trivia accuracy)
Write a trip report to trip_report.txt at the end of every game
Be architected with two classes — City and Player — with a reusable function_library.py module behind them

module1.label: Module 1: Getting Started
module1.intro_html: Establish the foundation: get input, do math, branch on choices. By the end of Module 1, your program asks the player’s name, simulates a single dice roll, lets them pick a destination, and tells them whether they made it — with a bonus detour to Galveston if they reach Houston.

labs.subtitle_html: Lab 1.1 • Ch 2 • Welcome to San Antonio
whatYouDo_html: Display a welcome banner and prompt for the player’s name with input()
Get a dice roll (1–20), convert to int, calculate miles (roll × 10)
Calculate miles remaining to each of four destinations (Corpus, Houston, Austin, Dallas)
Print a formatted Travel Report with all calculations
labs.result_html: The game greets the player, simulates one dice roll, and shows how many miles short of each Texas destination they’d be after rolling.

whatYouDo_html: Add a destination menu with 3 choices (Corpus, Houston, Austin)
Write an if/elif/else chain that sets destination, distance, status, message per choice
Check whether the player made it: miles_remaining >= 0
Use abs() to report how many miles short on a fail
Print a Trip Summary with the final result
labs.result_html: Player picks a destination. The program responds with a Trip Summary saying whether the roll was enough, or how many miles short.

whatYouDo_html: Add HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50 as a new named constant
Add a nested if inside the Houston branch: keep going to Galveston for the beach?
Use the or operator to accept both lower- and upper-case yes ('y' or 'Y')
Track a status variable across every branch: WINNER, SHORT, DETOUR, STOPPED, ERROR
Print a final message keyed off the status
labs.result_html: Houston now has a bonus leg to Galveston. Every branch ends with a tracked status — WINNER, SHORT, DETOUR, STOPPED, or ERROR — and a tailored end-of-trip message.

module2.label: Module 2: The Journey Begins
module2.subtitle_html: Chapters 4 & 5 • Loops, validation, your first module
module2.intro_html: Single-turn trips become multi-turn trips. The game loops over many turns, the dice roll changes mileage each turn, hazards appear, and you can play multiple games in one session. You also write your first reusable module — function_library.py — that every later lab will keep extending.

whatYouDo_html: Trim Lab 1.3: remove the dice roll, the Galveston detour, the win/short logic
Simplify to 3 destinations and add MILES_PER_TURN = 50
Add a while loop that drives toward the chosen destination
Track progress with a miles accumulator
Print turn-by-turn progress and a trip summary on arrival
labs.result_html: The game loops over turns until the player arrives. Each turn moves a fixed 50 miles. Turn-by-turn progress prints as the trip unfolds.

labs.subtitle_html: Lab 2.2 • Ch 5 • Texas Has Other Plans
whatYouDo_html: Create function_library.py with three helpers: flip_coin(), roll_d20(), check_percentage()
Import your own module into lab-2-2.py
Implement the Destination Gate: a coin flip decides if Texas lets you go where you want, otherwise you’re redirected
Replace fixed 50-mile travel with a D20 roll × 10 = miles per turn
Add hazard encounters at a 30% chance per turn
labs.result_html: Each turn rolls a D20 for mileage. A coin-flip gate may redirect you somewhere unexpected. Random hazards land on roughly 30% of turns. Your first reusable module is live.

whatYouDo_html: Add get_valid_int() and get_yes_no() to function_library.py (5 functions total)
Replace raw menu input with the validation function — bad input no longer crashes
Wrap the whole game in a sentinel-controlled play-again loop
Track cumulative session statistics across multiple games
labs.result_html: Input is validated — typing nonsense at a menu retries instead of crashing. The player can play game after game in one session, and the program tracks cumulative stats across all of them.

module3.label: Module 3: Keeping Track
module3.intro_html: The game gets a memory. You can save mid-trip, reload, and pick up where you left off. The destination menus collapse from if/elif chains into parallel lists. Hazards work the same way. Every game ends with a trip report file — a memento of the road trip you just played.

whatYouDo_html: Write save_game() that writes 9 fields of session state to a text file
Write load_game() that reads the file back, returns a 9-tuple, handles missing files
Add a main menu: New Game / Load Saved Game / Quit
Resume logic that picks up a saved session mid-trip and keeps session totals intact
Mid-trip 3-way option: Continue / Save and quit / Quit without saving
labs.result_html: Progress survives between sessions. Quit halfway through a trip, reload tomorrow, the game restarts exactly where you left off — including your session statistics.

labs.subtitle_html: Lab 3.2 • Ch 7 • Lists to the Rescue
whatYouDo_html: Replace destination if/elif chains with parallel lists: destinations, distances, notes (three per city)
Promote save_game() and load_game() from the lab file up to function_library.py
Add three universal helpers to the library: display_destinations(), get_destination(), get_redirect()
Build tracking lists: visited_cities (your route) and hazards_list (war stories)
Extend save/load to persist both tracking lists using a --LISTS_END-- separator
labs.result_html: The destination menu is data, not branches. The save file now preserves your full route and hazard history. The library is the right place to put reusable helpers.

whatYouDo_html: Add three more parallel lists for hazards: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS
Add get_random_hazard() to the library (parameterized — takes the hazard lists as arguments)
Refactor the in-game hazard block to call the library helper instead of inline if/elif
Sort hazards alphabetically in the trip summary using .copy() and .sort()
Count unique cities visited using the in operator
Build save_trip_report() — writes a formatted multi-section report to trip_report.txt at the end of every game
labs.result_html: Hazards work the same way as destinations now — parallel lists driven through library helpers. Every completed game saves a trip_report.txt the player can keep.

module4.label: Module 4: Smarter Code
module4.intro_html: The game becomes content-driven. City distances, city info, hazards, and Texas trivia all move out of code and into text files loaded at startup. Want to add a city, a hazard, or a trivia question? Add a row to a file. The Destination Gate retires in favor of a TRIVIA gate — players answer a TRUE/FALSE question about the city they want to visit, and a wrong answer reroutes them.

whatYouDo_html: Add load_city_distances(filename) to the library: parses CSV into a nested dictionary
Add load_city_info(filename): dictionary of dictionaries with type and tagline keys
Refactor display_destinations, get_destination, get_redirect to take (city, distances) instead of parallel lists
Add is_win_city and is_dead_end helpers reading from the city info dict
Rebuild lab-4-1.py: city graph loads from text files at startup; D20 mechanic and three-loop architecture preserved
labs.result_html: The city graph lives in city_distances.csv and city_info.csv. Add a row, add a city. No more recompiling code to add a destination.

whatYouDo_html: Add clean_input(prompt): return input(prompt).strip().upper()
Rewrite get_valid_int with .isdigit() — no more ValueError crashes on text input
Rewrite get_yes_no using clean_input — 'y'/'Y'/'n'/'N' collapses to one Y/N check
Add load_hazards(filename): parses hazards.txt into a list of dictionaries
Add handle_hazard(hazards): picks a random hazard, prints it, flips the escape coin, returns (name, escaped)
Replace the inline hazard block in lab-4-2.py with one call to the library helper
labs.result_html: Hazards now load from hazards.txt. Input handling is rock-solid — nonsense input retries instead of crashing. The function_library.py module is starting to look like a real toolkit.

whatYouDo_html: Add load_trivia(filename): parses trivia.txt into a dictionary keyed by city
Write trivia_gate(dest, trivia, current, distances) as a lab-local function: asks a TRUE/FALSE question, redirects on wrong
Replace the coin-flip Destination Gate in the in-game loop with a call to trivia_gate
Build unique_cities = set() and unique_hazards = set(); .add() entries as they happen
Replace the in-operator uniqueness loop and .copy().sort() hazard sort with sorted(unique_cities) / sorted(unique_hazards)
Track trivia_asked and trivia_correct; add a TRIVIA section to the trip report with accuracy percentage
labs.result_html: Wrong answer to a Texas trivia question — you get redirected somewhere else. Right answer — you head where you wanted. Sets handle uniqueness cleanly. The trip report grows a TRIVIA section.

module5.label: Module 5: Ship It
module5.intro_html: OOP arrives. City data and behavior bundle into a City class. The ten state variables tracking your road trip bundle into a Player class. The game plays exactly the same — but the code is now architected the way professional Python is written. This is your shippable artifact.

whatYouDo_html: Define a City class in city.py: __init__, private attributes, getters
Add connection methods, status methods, and __str__
Add enter_to_continue() to function_library.py as the library’s final function
Refactor lab-4-3.py into lab-5-1.py using City objects in place of the two dictionaries
labs.result_html: Cities are objects, not raw dicts. city.name, city.is_win_city(), city.distance_to('Austin'). The game plays identically; the code reads like a real codebase.

labs.subtitle_html: Lab 5.2 • Ch 10 • Deep in the Heart
whatYouDo_html: Define a Player class in player.py using the same pattern as City
Bundle all game state into one object: location, miles, cities visited, hazards, trivia counters
Use a list (for the full route) and a set (for unique cities) inside the class
Replace ten scattered state variables with a single player object
Follow the provided lab-5-2.py as a spec — every player. call signals a method to build
labs.result_html: One player object holds every piece of state about the road trip. Two classes (City + Player), one library module, four data files, and a game that ships. This is the portfolio piece.

finalProduct.label: The Final Product
finalProduct.subtitle_html: What you’ll have at the end of Module 5
finalProduct.body_html: Pull together everything you built. Your shipping artifact at the end of the semester is a complete, playable, OOP-architected text adventure game. It runs from the command line, has real content, real game mechanics, real input handling, and saves trip reports the player can keep.

byTheNumbers.label: Labs
byTheNumbers.sub: you carry each lab’s code forward

closingMentor.name: Danny Cavanaugh
closingMentor.quote_html: “Show this to a recruiter and say ‘I wrote this in my first programming class.’ Then watch their face.”
